items.q: Why do tropical cyclones only form over warm tropical oceans?
items.correct: They need sea surface temperatures above ~27 degrees C to a depth of 50m to provide energy through evaporation, plus enough Coriolis effect (away from the equator) to spin.
items.misconception: Hurricanes form wherever there are strong winds over the sea.
items.random_wrong: Hurricanes form when two cold fronts collide above the ocean.
items.why: Wind doesn't create hurricanes — warm ocean does. They draw energy from latent heat of evaporating seawater; that's why they weaken rapidly over land or cool water.

items.q: Why are tropical rainforest soils typically infertile?
items.correct: Heavy rainfall leaches nutrients, and most nutrients are stored in living biomass and rapidly recycled — the soil itself holds very little.
items.misconception: Because so much vegetation grows there, the soil must be fertile.
items.random_wrong: Because rainforest soils are made of clay.
items.why: The lush vegetation hides poor soil. The rainforest works as a tightly closed nutrient cycle; once cleared, the system collapses quickly because the soil cannot sustain growth.

items.q: What are the three cells of global atmospheric circulation?
items.correct: The Hadley cell (equator to 30 degrees), Ferrel cell (30 to 60 degrees) and Polar cell (60 degrees to poles) together redistribute heat from equator to poles.
items.misconception: There is one big Hadley cell from the equator to each pole.
items.random_wrong: The three cells are tropical, temperate and arctic, named after climate zones.
items.why: Students often learn 'Hadley' as the only cell. There are three in each hemisphere, and their boundaries (at 30 and 60 degrees) explain global pressure belts, deserts and the polar front.

items.q: What is the minimum SST needed for tropical cyclone formation?
items.why: Correct answer: 26.5degC. “20degC” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Category 5. “Category 3” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Approximately how many people died in Typhoon Haiyan?
items.why: The real figure is 6300. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
